---
title: "Quiz on Linux Foundations"
author: "Nguyen MTS"
date: "2026-05-11"
format:
  html:
    css: "../../website-assets/quiz.css"
---

```{=html}
<script src="../../website-assets/quiz.js"></script>
<script>
    const mcqFeedback = {
        mcq_e1: {
            A: "ls lists files, it doesn't show your current path.",
            B: "Correct. 'pwd' stands for Print Working Directory.",
            C: "cd is for changing directories, not printing the current one.",
            D: "whereami is not a standard Linux command."
        },
        mcq_e2: {
            A: "/ is the root directory, the very top of the tree.",
            B: ". refers to the current directory.",
            C: "Correct. The tilde (~) represents the user's home directory.",
            D: ".. refers to the parent directory (one level up)."
        },
        mcq_e3: {
            A: "mv moves or renames a file.",
            B: "Correct. 'cp' stands for copy.",
            C: "rm removes/deletes a file.",
            D: "ln creates links between files."
        },
        mcq_e4: {
            A: "Correct. 'man' opens the manual page for a command.",
            B: "--help provides a short help menu, but 'man' is the full manual.",
            C: "help mkdir is used for internal shell commands, not all programs.",
            D: "mkdir man would try to create a directory named 'man'."
        },
        mcq_e5: {
            A: "ls is the command.",
            B: "microgen2026/ is the argument (the target directory).",
            C: "Correct. Options (or flags) usually start with a dash and modify the command's behavior.",
            D: "The target is the argument, not the option."
        },
        mcq_i1: {
            A: "cp without -r will skip directories.",
            B: "Correct. The -r (recursive) option is required to copy directories and their contents.",
            C: "mv moves the directory instead of copying it.",
            D: "This copies the files inside, but not necessarily hidden files or the folder itself."
        },
        mcq_i2: {
            A: "The wildcard * matches any sequence of characters, including '10'.",
            B: "Correct. The wildcard ? matches exactly one single character, matching A and B but skipping 10.",
            C: "This matches only single digits 0-9.",
            D: "This matches uppercase letters and any sequence after, but syntax is slightly off."
        },
        mcq_i3: {
            A: "-a lists hidden files but doesn't make sizes human-readable.",
            B: "-lh makes sizes human-readable but misses hidden files.",
            C: "Correct. -l is long format, -h is human-readable, and -a includes hidden files.",
            D: "-H usually resolves symbolic links, it doesn't do human-readable."
        },
        mcq_i4: {
            A: "Correct. The -c flag counts the matching lines directly.",
            B: "count is not a real command.",
            C: "wc -l cannot take a search string argument like this.",
            D: "find is for locating files by name in the filesystem, not text matching inside them."
        },
        mcq_i5: {
            A: "Both manage standard output by default.",
            B: "Correct. > overwrites, destroying old data. >> appends, adding to existing data.",
            C: "Both can create new files if they don't exist.",
            D: "Both handle text streams."
        },
        mcq_i6: {
            A: "less is a viewer, not an editor.",
            B: "Correct. less allows efficient scrolling by only loading the visible part of the file, saving RAM.",
            C: "less doesn't automatically unzip, zless does.",
            D: "less doesn't color-code DNA natively."
        },
        mcq_i7: {
            A: "wc will pipe to grep, it's just logically flawed for our goal.",
            B: "Pipes pass output left-to-right, they are not reversible.",
            C: "Correct. The left command counts the total lines and outputs a number. grep then searches that single number for '2024'.",
            D: "No, wc just outputs line counts."
        },
        mcq_i8: {
            A: "install adds new software to an environment.",
            B: "Correct. You activate an environment to enter it.",
            C: "go is not a Conda/Mamba command.",
            D: "start is not a Conda/Mamba command."
        },
        mcq_i9: {
            A: "cut defaults to tab-delimited, so this fails on a CSV.",
            B: "Correct. You must specify the comma delimiter with -d ','.",
            C: "column is for formatting layout, not simple extraction.",
            D: "grep doesn't have a -col flag for columns."
        },
        mcq_i10: {
            A: "This extracts ONLY the header lines, doing the exact opposite of what you want.",
            B: "This cuts the columns first, then searches for '#', which may incorrectly exclude rows if the second column happens to start with `#`.",
            C: "Correct. This filters out the headers using -v and passes the remaining lines directly to cut.",
            D: "You must pipe `|` between commands; `>` is for writing to files, not passing data."
        },
        mcq_a1: {
            A: "nohup helps, but tmux/screen is designed specifically to maintain active, attachable sessions.",
            B: "Correct. tmux or screen hosts your shell session, keeping it alive continuously even if SSH drops.",
            C: "A bash script alone stops running if the parent terminal is disconnected.",
            D: "Running as root doesn't stop the connection from dropping."
        },
        mcq_a2: {
            A: "A for loop works, but only processes one file at a time sequentially.",
            B: "cat merges everything, giving you one massive total but not file-level counts.",
            C: "Correct. GNU parallel efficiently distributes the grep jobs across multiple CPU cores simultaneously.",
            D: "This works but is incredibly manual and time consuming."
        },
        mcq_a3: {
            A: "Correct. >> appends standard output, and 2>&1 merges standard error into the same output stream.",
            B: "This separates the error and overwrites standard output.",
            C: "You cannot pipe directly to a file name without commands like tee.",
            D: "Invalid syntax for redirection."
        },
        mcq_a4: {
            A: "uniq only compares adjacent lines, so un-sorted identical lines are missed.",
            B: "Correct. Sorting the file groups identical lines together so uniq can successfully filter them before counting.",
            C: "grep doesn't have a -u unique flag.",
            D: "wc counts lines, returning a single number, which uniq cannot process further."
        },
        mcq_a5: {
            A: "This works but does not match exactly our specific sample. Sample like 'SRR24679994_1' would still be matched.",
            B: "Variable interpolation inside variables does not work with spaces like $MY SAMPLE without braces or quotes, causing syntax errors.",
            C: "You do not use $ on the left side of the equals sign when setting a variable in Bash.",
            D: "Correct. This properly defines the local variable and securely protects the variable from word-splitting using double quotes in grep."
        }
    };
</script>
```

**Instructions:** Answer all 20 questions below. When finished, click **Submit Quiz** at the bottom. You need at least **16 correct answers (80%)** to pass.

::: {.callout-note}
## Quiz Structure
- **Questions 1-5**: Level 1 (Remembering - Recalling facts and basic concepts)
- **Questions 6-15**: Levels 2-4 (Understanding to Analyzing - Explaining ideas, applying knowledge, and drawing connections)
- **Questions 16-20**: Levels 5-6 (Evaluating and Creating - Justifying decisions and producing pipelines)
:::

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_e1" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">1. Which command reveals your current location in the file system?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e1" value="A"> A. <code>ls</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e1" value="B"> B. <code>pwd</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e1" value="C"> C. <code>cd</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e1" value="D"> D. <code>whereami</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_e2" data-correct="C">
  <div class="question">2. Which character represents your <strong>Home</strong> directory?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e2" value="A"> A. <code>/</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e2" value="B"> B. <code>.</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e2" value="C"> C. <code>~</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e2" value="D"> D. <code>..</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_e3" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">3. What command is used to <strong>copy</strong> a file?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e3" value="A"> A. <code>mv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e3" value="B"> B. <code>cp</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e3" value="C"> C. <code>rm</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e3" value="D"> D. <code>ln</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_e4" data-correct="A">
  <div class="question">4. How do you find the manual page for the <code>mkdir</code> command?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e4" value="A"> A. <code>man mkdir</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e4" value="B"> B. <code>mkdir --help</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e4" value="C"> C. <code>help mkdir</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e4" value="D"> D. <code>mkdir man</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_e5" data-correct="C">
  <div class="question">5. In the command <code>ls -l microgen2026/</code>, what do we call the <code>-l</code> part?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e5" value="A"> A. The command</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e5" value="B"> B. The argument</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e5" value="C"> C. The option (or flag)</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_e5" value="D"> D. The target</label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i1" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">6. How do you copy the directory <code>day-0/</code> AND all its contents to your <code>backup/</code> directory?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i1" value="A"> A. <code>cp day-0/ backup/</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i1" value="B"> B. <code>cp -r day-0/ backup/</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i1" value="C"> C. <code>mv day-0/ backup/</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i1" value="D"> D. <code>cp day-0/\* backup/</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i2" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">7. Which file wildcard pattern matches <code>sample_A.fasta</code> and <code>sample_B.fasta</code>, but NOT <code>sample_10.fasta</code> ?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i2" value="A"> A. <code>sample_\*.fasta</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i2" value="B"> B. <code>sample_?.fasta</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i2" value="C"> C. <code>sample_[0-9].fasta</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i2" value="D"> D. <code>sample_[A-Z]\*.fasta</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i3" data-correct="C">
  <div class="question">8. How do you display all files (including hidden ones) with <strong>human-readable</strong> sizes?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i3" value="A"> A. <code>ls -a</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i3" value="B"> B. <code>ls -lh</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i3" value="C"> C. <code>ls -lah</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i3" value="D"> D. <code>ls -H</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i4" data-correct="A">
  <div class="question">9. How do you search for the word "PASS" inside <code>val.vcf</code> and output only the <strong>count</strong> of matching lines?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i4" value="A"> A. <code>grep -c "PASS" val.vcf</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i4" value="B"> B. <code>grep "PASS" val.vcf | count</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i4" value="C"> C. <code>wc -l "PASS" val.vcf</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i4" value="D"> D. <code>find "PASS" val.vcf</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i5" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">10. What is the critical difference between <code>></code> and <code>>></code> when redirecting output to a file?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i5" value="A"> A. <code>></code> redirects standard output, while <code>>></code> redirects errors.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i5" value="B"> B. <code>></code> overwrites the destination file, while <code>>></code> appends to it.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i5" value="C"> C. <code>></code> creates a new file, while <code>>></code> only works if the file exists.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i5" value="D"> D. <code>></code> is for text streams, while <code>>></code> is for binary data.</label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i6" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">11. Why is <code>less</code> generally preferred over <code>cat</code> or <code>nano</code> for viewing large genomic data files?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i6" value="A"> A. <code>less</code> can actively edit files line-by-line.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i6" value="B"> B. <code>less</code> allows efficient scrolling without loading the entire giant file into memory at once.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i6" value="C"> C. <code>less</code> automatically unzips compressed files.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i6" value="D"> D. <code>less</code> color-codes true DNA base pairs natively.</label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i7" data-correct="C">
  <div class="question">12. Consider the pipeline: <code>grep "2024" metadata.csv | wc -l</code>. What happens if you mistakenly reverse it to <code>wc -l metadata.csv | grep "2024"</code>?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i7" value="A"> A. The command fails entirely because <code>wc</code> cannot pipe to <code>grep</code>.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i7" value="B"> B. It successfully counts the lines since UNIX pipelines are reversible.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i7" value="C"> C. It outputs the total lines first, then <code>grep</code> searches that single number output for '2024', failing the intent.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i7" value="D"> D. It prints all lines from 2024 and then adds a total sum at the very end.</label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i8" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">13. How do you enter the isolated "bubble" of a Conda/Mamba environment named <code>qc</code>?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i8" value="A"> A. <code>mamba install qc</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i8" value="B"> B. <code>mamba activate qc</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i8" value="C"> C. <code>conda go qc</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i8" value="D"> D. <code>mamba start qc</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i9" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">14. How do you successfully extract only the 5th column from a comma-separated file named <code>data.csv</code>?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i9" value="A"> A. <code>cut -f 5 data.csv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i9" value="B"> B. <code>cut -f 5 -d "," data.csv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i9" value="C"> C. <code>column -5 data.csv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i9" value="D"> D. <code>grep -col 5 data.csv</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_i10" data-correct="C">
  <div class="question">15. How do you successfully extract only the 2nd and 4th columns from <code>965Kp.vcf</code> while actively ignoring all header lines that start with <code>#</code>?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i10" value="A"> A. <code>grep "^#" 965Kp.vcf | cut -f 2,4</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i10" value="B"> B. <code>cut -f 2,4 965Kp.vcf | grep -v "^#"</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i10" value="C"> C. <code>grep -v "^#" 965Kp.vcf | cut -f 2,4</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_i10" value="D"> D. <code>cut -f 2,4 965Kp.vcf > grep -v "^#"</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_a1" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">16. You need to download large genome files, and your SSH connection is highly unstable. Which approach is the most robust to prevent job termination?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a1" value="A"> A. Use <code>nohup</code> on the <code>wget</code> command.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a1" value="B"> B. Run the download script inside a <code>tmux</code> or <code>screen</code> session.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a1" value="C"> C. Create a Bash script and add executable permissions.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a1" value="D"> D. Run the commands in a superuser (root) environment.</label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_a2" data-correct="C">
  <div class="question">17. You must count the number of FASTA sequences (<code>&gt;</code>) across 100 different files. Which method is the most efficient on a multi-core server?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a2" value="A"> A. Use a <code>for</code> loop to iterate through each file sequentially.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a2" value="B"> B. Use <code>cat \*.fasta | grep "&gt;" | wc -l</code>.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a2" value="C"> C. Use <code>ls \*.fasta | parallel -j 8 grep -c '&gt;' {}</code>.</label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a2" value="D"> D. Use <code>grep -c "&gt;"</code> separately in 100 different terminal windows.</label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_a3" data-correct="A">
  <div class="question">18. You are designing a script that appends its system hardware summary to <code>system_log.txt</code> and captures any identical stream errors to the same file. Synthesize the right command:</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a3" value="A"> A. <code>lscpu >> system_log.txt 2>&1</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a3" value="B"> B. <code>lscpu > system_log.txt 2> error.txt</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a3" value="C"> C. <code>lscpu | system_log.txt 2>&1</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a3" value="D"> D. <code>lscpu >> system_log.txt < 2</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_a4" data-correct="B">
  <div class="question">19. You need to find the number of <strong>unique</strong> species in <code>samples.txt</code> (one species name per line). Based on the properties of <code>uniq</code>, how would you construct this pipeline?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a4" value="A"> A. <code>uniq samples.txt | wc -l</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a4" value="B"> B. <code>sort samples.txt | uniq | wc -l</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a4" value="C"> C. <code>grep -u samples.txt | wc -l</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a4" value="D"> D. <code>wc -l samples.txt | uniq</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="mcq" id="mcq_a5" data-correct="D">
  <div class="question">20. You are writing an automation script and need to store the sample name 'SRR24679994' as a variable to search for it inside <code>metadata_carbResist.tsv</code>. Which sequence perfectly creates and evaluates this variable?</div>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a5" value="A"> A. <code>MY_SAMPLE="SRR24679994" ; grep "\$MY_SAMPLE" metadata_carbResist.tsv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a5" value="B"> B. <code>MY_SAMPLE="SRR24679994" ; grep -w \$MY SAMPLE metadata_carbResist.tsv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a5" value="C"> C. <code>\$MY_SAMPLE="SRR24679994" ; grep MY_SAMPLE metadata_carbResist.tsv</code></label>
  <label><input type="radio" name="mcq_a5" value="D"> D. <code>MY_SAMPLE="SRR24679994" ; grep -w "\$MY_SAMPLE" metadata_carbResist.tsv</code></label>
  <div class="feedback"></div>
</div>

<div class="quiz-controls">
  <button class="btn-submit" onclick="submitQuiz()">Submit Quiz</button>
  <button class="btn-reset" onclick="resetQuiz()">Reset Quiz</button>
</div>

<div id="mcq-total-score-display" style="display:none;"></div>
